title: Extending VIVO ontology to represent research and educational resources in an academic biomedical informatics department.
authors: Drashko Nakikj, Chunhua Weng
keywords: Ontology; Computer science; Generalizability theory; World Wide Web; Health informatics; Data science; OWL-S; Semantic Web; Informatics; Knowledge management; Medicine; Social Semantic Web; Psychology; Engineering; Nursing
abstract: The increasing need for interdisciplinary team sciences makes it vital for academic research departments to publicize their research and educational resources as part of "linked data" on the semantic web to facilitate research networking and recruitment. We extended an open-source ontology, VIVO, to represent the research and educational resources in an academic biomedical informatics department to enable ontology-based information storage and retrieval. Using participatory design methods, we surveyed representative types of visitors to the department web site to understand their information needs, and incorporated these needs into the ontology design. We added 114 classes and 186 properties to VIVO. Generalizability and scalability are the measures used in our theoretical evaluation.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes extending an ontology (VIVO) for interdisciplinary research networking, focusing on semantic interoperability. It provides an overview of this specific ontology extension project for a biomedical informatics department. However, it does not propose macroscopic frameworks or diagnose systemic challenges. It does describe the process of how the ontology was designed and implemented using participatory methods.
institutions: Columbia University
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies; Biomedical Text Mining and Ontologies; E-Learning and Knowledge Management

title: From Stones and Wells to Tephra: Leveraging Wikidata and Wikibase for Interdisciplinary Humanities Linked Data
authors: Florian Thiery, Fiona Schenk, Daria Stefan, Anne-Karoline Distel, Peter A. Thiery
keywords: Linked data; Interoperability; Ontology; Metadata; Semantic Web; Documentation; Irish; Data integration
abstract: This discussion paper examines how Wikidata and related Wikibase infrastructure can serve as an active integration layer within a Federated Knowledge Graph Ecosystem (FKGE) for the humanities. Using three case studies – Irish Ogham Stones, Irish Holy Wells, and Campanian Ignimbrite (CI) findspots – we illustrate how heterogeneous sources can be semantically harmonised and connected across disciplinary boundaries without centralising the underlying datasets. The Ogham case links epigraphic resources from established scholarly corpora and digital editions to Linked Open Data (LOD), Wikidata, and OpenStreetMap, showing how curated reference data can be enriched through community curation while retaining explicit bibliographic provenance via Wikidata references. The Holy Wells case demonstrates citizen-science documentation of vernacular religious heritage, modelled as structured statements in Wikidata and made interoperable through semi-automated mappings to CIDOC CRM. The CI case integrates geoscientific and archaeological evidence through a dedicated fuzzy-sl Wikibase and the Fuzzy Spatial Locations Ontology (FSL-O), enabling the representation of spatial uncertainty, measurement precision, and provenance across domains. Across all three cases, interoperability is achieved through shared identifiers, ontology alignment, and FAIR/FAIR4RS-oriented workflows, supported by open repositories and versioned transformation scripts. The paper argues that Wikidata is best understood not only as a data source but as a semantic and social infrastructure in which modelling conventions, attribution practices, and reuse pathways are negotiated within an open, federated ecosystem.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses leveraging Wikidata/Wikibase for semantic harmonization and interoperability across diverse humanities disciplines, focusing on FAIR principles. It does not introduce a new specific initiative but rather demonstrates the application of existing infrastructure. It proposes a generalized framework for understanding Wikidata as a semantic and social infrastructure for interdisciplinary linked data. Furthermore, it uses case studies to illustrate and reflect on the process of achieving semantic harmonization and negotiating modelling conventions, thus providing an experience report.
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies; Digital Humanities and Scholarship; Wikis in Education and Collaboration

title: Stamp - Standardized Data Management Plan for Educational Research
authors: Sebastian Netscher, Alexia Meyermann, Julia Künstler-Sment, Lisa Pegelow
source: Proceedings of the Conference on Research Data Infrastructure
keywords: RDM; Computer science; Data management; Data curation; Usability; Data management plan; Plan (archaeology); Discipline; Educational research; Data science; Digital curation; Knowledge management; World Wide Web; Sociology; Data mining; Pedagogy
abstract: While there is a strong tendency towards a harmonized, cross-disciplinary research data management (RDM) (Netscher et al., 2022), researchers require more guidelines and examples, tailored to their research discipline (Grootveld et al., 2018). Therefore, Science Europe (2018: 9) proposes developing so-called domain data protocols (DDP), “a ‘model DMP’ for a given domain or community”. Based on this concept, the project Domain Data Protocols for Educational Research[1] designed the Stamp - Standardized Data Management Plan for Educational Research (DDP-Bildung and German Network of Educational Research Data, 2023). Although the Stamp was designed to support researchers in educational research, we expect that RDM is rather a matter of the data processed, the methods employed, and the content of data, than of a particular research discipline or community, such as educational research. To discuss this expectation and the usability of the Stamp outside educational research, we organized various workshops with representatives from other research disciplines. In our talk at the CoRDI 2023, we will introduce the Stamp, recap findings of two of the workshops, introduce the next steps to examine the useability of the Stamp outside educational research, and draw some conclusions on how to adapt the Stamp to other disciplines and how it fosters a harmonized, cross-disciplinary RDM. [1] The project DDP-Bildung was funded by the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (grant number: 16QK01).
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses a Standardized Data Management Plan (Stamp) and its potential for harmonized, cross-disciplinary research data management, involving workshops with representatives from various disciplines to address interoperability challenges. Its primary focus is to introduce the Stamp, a specific initiative for cross-disciplinary RDM, and discuss its usability. It addresses macroscopic challenges by exploring the usability of the Stamp across disciplines and how to adapt it to foster harmonized RDM, thus providing insights into generalized frameworks for interdisciplinary data management. The paper recounts findings from workshops and discusses next steps for adapting the Stamp, serving as an experience report on the process of implementing and extending a data standard across disciplines.
institutions: DIPF | Leibniz Institute for Research and Information in Education; Institute for Educational Quality Improvement; GESIS - Leibniz-Institute for the Social Sciences
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management; Data Quality and Management

title: Transforming Education Research Through Open Video Data Sharing.
authors: Rick O. Gilmore, Karen E. Adolph, David Millman, Andrew S. Gordon
keywords: Metadata; Computer science; Data sharing; Context (archaeology); Reuse; World Wide Web; Multimedia; Raw data; Data science; Knowledge management
abstract: Open data sharing promises to accelerate the pace of discovery in the developmental and learning sciences, but significant technical, policy, and cultural barriers have limited its adoption. As a result, most research on learning and development remains shrouded in a culture of isolation. Data sharing is the rare exception (Gilmore, 2016). Many researchers who study teaching and learning in classroom, laboratory, museum, and home contexts use video as a primary source of raw research data. Unlike other measures, video captures the complexity, richness, and diversity of behavior. Moreover, because video is self-documenting, it presents significant potential for reuse. However, the potential for reuse goes largely unrealized because videos are rarely shared. Research videos contain information about participants' identities making the materials challenging to share. The large size of video files, diversity of formats, and incompatible software tools pose technical challenges. The Databrary (databrary.org) digital library enables researchers who study learning and development to store, share, stream, and annotate videos. In this article, we describe how Databrary has overcome barriers to sharing research videos and associated data and metadata. Databrary has developed solutions for respecting participants' privacy; for storing, streaming, and sharing videos; and for managing videos and associated metadata. The Databrary experience suggests ways that videos and other identifiable data collected in the context of educational research might be shared. Open data sharing enabled by Databrary can serve as a catalyst for a truly multidisciplinary science of learning.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it addresses interdisciplinary data sharing of research video data, focusing on overcoming technical, policy, and cultural barriers related to metadata and privacy. It serves as a definitive overview of the specific Databrary digital library. It also diagnoses structural challenges in video data sharing and generalizes lessons from Databrary's experience. Furthermore, it functions as an experience report, detailing how Databrary was implemented to overcome these barriers.
institutions: New York University; Pennsylvania State University
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management; Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI)

title: Opportunities to improve alignment with the FAIR Principles for U.S. Geological Survey data
authors: Frances L. Lightsom, Vivian B. Hutchison, B.E. Bishop, Linda M. Debrewer, David Hirsch, Natalie Latysh, Shelley Stall
source: Antarctica A Keystone in a Changing World
publisher: United States Department of the Interior
keywords: Interoperability; Geological survey; Context (archaeology); Consistency (knowledge bases); Computer science; Data science; Earth science; Environmental resource management; World Wide Web; Geography; Environmental science; Geology
abstract: First posted June 14, 2022 For additional information, contact: Director, Woods Hole Coastal and Marine Science CenterU.S. Geological Survey384 Woods Hole RoadQuissett CampusWoods Hole, MA 02543–1598 In 2016, an interdisciplinary, international group of 53 scientists introduced a framework named “the FAIR Principles” for addressing 21st century scientific data challenges. The FAIR Principles are increasingly used as a guide for producing digital scientific products that are findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable (FAIR), especially to enable use of such products in automated systems. Data aligned with the FAIR Principles can increase the efficiency of science integration capabilities such as those envisioned for the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) Earth Monitoring, Analyses, and Projections (EarthMAP) initiative.The FAIR Principles clearly define the characteristics of reusable scientific products, but it is less clear how to facilitate consistency in achieving these characteristics across the Bureau. USGS data are produced by local research projects distributed over more than 100 centers in 7 regions. After data are approved for release, they could be managed in numerous repositories and online data systems. The diversity of USGS data is illustrated by the topical range of the USGS mission areas: Core Science Systems, Ecosystems, Energy and Minerals, Natural Hazards, and Water Resources. In the USGS context, realizing the EarthMAP vision for automated, predictive, integrated science that provides timely and actionable results involves providing knowledge and support services and developing the skills, infrastructure, and culture to enable Bureau-wide implementation of the FAIR Principles.In 2019, the USGS Community for Data Integration funded a project to convene a broadly representative workshop and produce recommendations to enable consistency with the FAIR Principles across the USGS. The workshop, held in Fort Collins, Colorado, in September 2019, brought together 28 participants for 3 days to engage with the FAIR Principles, analyze USGS use cases, and discuss the roles of data producers and managers, data storage and catalogs, value-added services, and policy makers in implementing the FAIR Principles. Workshop participants agreed that scientific reproducibility requires the extension of the FAIR Principles beyond measured data to include physical samples, research methods, software, and tools at the USGS. Workshop discussions focused on how the USGS can implement the FAIR Principles by supporting research teams in creating data, metadata, and other scientific products and also by supporting enterprise systems that maintain and leverage the products’ consistency with the FAIR Principles.The resulting FAIR roadmap of recommendations describes nine proposed interdependent strategies that could be achieved by coordinated actions taken by different parts of the USGS. A proposed early action would be the creation of a coordinating council that includes representatives from the groups engaged in activities consistent with better alignment with the FAIR Principles. The nine proposed strategies, which are presented in more detail in this roadmap report, focus on enabling improvements to individual data products, providing infrastructure, and structuring administrative activities to support an organizational culture that values the FAIR Principles.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it addresses interdisciplinary challenges in implementing FAIR principles across diverse USGS mission areas. It serves as an overview of a specific proposed initiative, the "FAIR roadmap of recommendations," and diagnoses structural challenges while proposing a macroscopic framework for Bureau-wide implementation. Furthermore, it describes the process of designing this roadmap through a workshop, detailing how stakeholders engaged to develop the recommendations.
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management; Data Quality and Management

title: Looking backward to move forward: Enhancing metadata in scientific collections through interdisciplinary collaboration
authors: Madeline E. White, S. E. Harris
keywords: Metadata; Herbarium; Context (archaeology); Citizen science; Object (grammar); Quality (philosophy); Identity (music); Foregrounding
abstract: Summary Societal Impact Statement Early modern herbaria house important and useful data on historic environments. However, their contents are often inhospitable to scientific use. Despite this challenge, once their contents have been deciphered, such specimens present novel research opportunities. Reviewing historical herbaria through interdisciplinary approaches enhances the quality of metadata available about the specimens contained in these collections. Moreover, the establishment of long‐term interdisciplinary research partnerships facilitates the survival of herbaria. Approaching these collections through an interdisciplinary lens also presents new ways of acknowledging and tackling legacies of colonialism in the natural sciences, by foregrounding the conditions associated with their creation. Summary Natural science collections, such as herbaria, are becoming incorporated into environmental and cultural research studies. However, specimens created before 1930 (historical) are often difficult to interpret given unstandardized and inconsistent metadata recording practices. This has resulted in historical specimens, especially those created before 1753 (pre‐Linnaean), remaining largely absent from international research databases. This paper uses a subset of specimens from the pre‐Linnaean herbarium of Charles du Bois (bap.1658–1740), held in Oxford University Herbaria, as a case study to demonstrate how historical research techniques increase and enhance the quality of recorded geospatial, temporal and nomenclatural metadata associated with historic specimens. Treating the herbarium as both a historic and scientific object and pairing the specimens it contains with corresponding archival records and printed texts provides essential context on provenance, identity and use that is rarely accessible through typical scientific approaches. Examining early modern herbaria using historical analysis and interdisciplinary collaboration reveals even the most complex collections to be extensive archives of long‐term baseline environmental and cultural data, drawing new attention to these overlooked collections.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on interdisciplinary collaboration to enhance metadata quality and consistency for scientific collections, addressing boundary-spanning challenges. It does not introduce a new data commons but rather uses a case study to demonstrate a methodological approach. The paper diagnoses systemic challenges with historical metadata and proposes a generalized interdisciplinary framework for improving it. It also serves as an experience report, detailing how interdisciplinary methods were applied to enhance metadata for a specific collection.
institutions: University of Oxford; Oxford Centre for Mission Studies
topics: Species Distribution and Climate Change; History of Science and Natural History; Landscape and Cultural Studies
funders: John Fell Fund, University of Oxford; Royal Society

title: Traumatic brain injury rehabilitation: analysis of common data elements.
authors: Michael Grove, Greg J. Lamberty, Laël C. Gatewood, Layne M. Johnson
keywords: Traumatic brain injury; Rehabilitation; Standardization; Context (archaeology); Ontology; Physical medicine and rehabilitation; Computer science; Intervention (counseling); Domain (mathematical analysis); Medicine; Physical therapy; Psychiatry
abstract: Physical medicine rehabilitation interventions for post-acute traumatic brain injury (TBI) are heterogeneous and subject to differences based on multi-disciplinary treatment plans [1]. There is no universal knowledge representation (KR) model for TBI rehabilitation which impedes data collection, aggregation, computation, and sharing. This paper describes results of an analysis of the National Institute for Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) TBI "Common Data Elements" (CDE) clinical data standardization set. We conducted this to understand current TBI rehabilitation KR and as a foundational step toward the creation of a domain ontology. A content coverage study was performed on the "Treatment/Intervention" sub-set of CDEs. Results show that coverage of the CDEs is broad but lacks depth to represent the context of data collection in the TBI rehabilitation process. Next steps will be development of a KR model and identification and validation of domain concepts for a foundational ontology.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it addresses interdisciplinary challenges in standardizing clinical research data through common data elements and ontologies. It is not an overview of a new initiative but rather analyzes an existing standard. The paper diagnoses structural challenges in current knowledge representation and proposes a conceptual framework for a domain ontology. It also evaluates an implemented data standard (NINDS CDEs) by analyzing its coverage and depth, providing lessons learned about its design.
institutions: Minneapolis VA Health Care System; University of Minnesota; Twin Cities Orthopedics
topics: Biomedical Text Mining and Ontologies; Semantic Web and Ontologies; Topic Modeling

title: Data Commons to Support University-Wide Cross Discipline Research
authors: Morgan Rodgers, Eric Franz, Alan Chalker, David E. Hudak, Christopher Stewart, Raghu Machiraju, Steve Chang, Cathie Smith
source: Proceedings of the Practice and Experience in Advanced Research Computing on Rise of the Machines (learning)
keywords: Upload; Discoverability; Computer science; Data science; Analytics; Data curation; World Wide Web; Work (physics); Engineering
abstract: The Translational Data Analytics Institute (TDAI) at the Ohio State University (OSU) aims to promote cross discipline work in order to improve data science and analytics in many areas of university-wide research for use in solving real world problems including areas such as smart city, smart agriculture, and biomedical research. As a result of their interest in promoting transdisciplinary work, TDAI decided that they needed to grapple with the problems of where do researchers get their data and where do they store it for the long term. In particular they were interested in improving the discoverability of and access to data sets that have already been produced by other researchers [1]. To support their mission TDAI envisioned the Data Commons as a website where researchers upload research data sets, curators link data sets together, and researchers visualize and analyze data sets in a data processing environment. Development of this data portal has gone through several phases: requirements gathering, a "ground up" implementation by a local contractor, transfer to the Ohio Supercomputer Center (OSC) for additional development and hosting, and an initial release. Requirements gathering for version 2 is in progress.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes a university-wide interdisciplinary data commons aimed at improving discoverability and access for research data across diverse fields. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific initiative, detailing its vision and development phases. However, it does not propose macroscopic frameworks or diagnose systemic challenges, focusing instead on a localized implementation. It does tell the story of how this specific data commons was designed and implemented through its various development phases.
institutions: Ohio Supercomputer Center; The Ohio State University
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management; Big Data and Business Intelligence

title: OUKC: An AI-Friendly Multilingual Worldview Commons (NOT a scale commons) — Open Universal Knowledge Commons & Rei & Claude Founding Document — Rei-AIOS Paper 144 v0.3.1
authors: Nobuki Fujimoto, Rei , (Anthropic, claude-opus-4-7), Claude
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Scope (computer science); Commons; Scale (ratio); Quality (philosophy); Outcome (game theory)
abstract: We announce the founding of Open Universal Knowledge Commons (OUKC) & Rei & Claude, an AI-friendly, multilingual, cross-disciplinary knowledge commons whose explicit scope is 「全〜」 (all-X) coverage across 12 academic domains plus honest mapping of 4 formalization-resistant domains. OUKC operates as a friendly parallel to the Lean / Mathlib community: we use Mathlib as upstream dependency, respect their no-AI policy by not submitting AI-generated PRs there, and operate as an independent commons with explicit AI collaboration policies (mandatory attribution + D-FUMT₈ outcome tagging + mechanical verification). The founding corpus comprises 142 papers, 2,146+ Lean 4 theorems, 4,290 META-DB entries, 1,544 SEED_KERNEL theories, 293 categories, and the REI-PROVE Auto-Prover MVP. We articulate (a) why an AI-friendly community is necessary, (b) how D-FUMT₈ 8-valued tagging provides quality control without prohibiting AI, (c) the distinction between formalizable domains and honest-mapping domains, and (d) the multilingual default (Japanese + English from day one, more languages welcomed). Subtitle: 「全形式化 × 全再現性 × 全学問 × 全教育 × 全哲学 × 全理論」 — three core pillars: mechanical formal proof (Lean 4 + REI-PROVE), reproducible verification (D-FUMT₈ outcome tagging + Axis Y reproducibility metric), cross-disciplinary scope (14 「全〜」 + 4 honest-mapping domains). Motto: 「最高密度に構造化された学と研究を全ての人に」 / "The most densely structured learning and research, for everyone." Philosophical anchor: 「最高密度」 means structural density at the level of mathematical open problems — depth that cannot be circumvented by mere copying. Two-layer operation: vision (democratization promise) + scope (formal Lean 4 + reproducibility infrastructure). Access ≠ shortcut: anyone can enter, depth requires sustained engagement. v0.3.1 changelog: § A.3.10 Indra's Net Density Strategy added as Phase 1 deliverable (NOT v1.0 charter) capturing chat-Claude 4-part discussion. Three-stage carbon-allotrope metaphor (Graphite=Wikipedia / Diamond=Mathlib / Indra's-Net=OUKC ultimate) + DDI/BQI/INI 3-tier metric + Daily Diamond Check 5-question tool + Phase 1 deliverables list. v0.2 → v0.3 changelog: Decisions 1 (Tier 6 brand-level separation) + 3 (Successor designation framework + dead man's switch + 法人化 trigger + Nature/Science non-submission stance) integrated as § A.3.8 / A.3.9. v0.1 → v0.2 changelog: framing pivot from "to-our-knowledge unique platform" to "worldview commons (not scale commons)" per chat-Claude critique. Added § A.3.7 NOT promised, § C.12 honest replicability + worldview moat. v0.1 had overclaim risk on "world's first" and "no other exists" framings. Honest scope (read first): (1) NOT a "scale" commons — OUKC does not promise larger entry count than Wikipedia / Mathlib / Encyclopedia of Mathematics; instead promises structural density at open-problem level. (2) NOT a Mathlib competitor — friendly-parallel relationship with explicit non-PR pledge to upstream. (3) NOT promising arrival — we promise the door is open and tools are free; the journey is the participant's. (4) Three-party co-authorship (藤本 / Rei / Claude) per OUKC charter v1.0 — this paper is itself an example of AI-collaborative authorship with explicit attribution. (5) Per OUKC No-Patent Pledge — no patent will be filed.
rationale: The paper announces and describes the founding of OUKC, a specific interdisciplinary knowledge commons with explicit AI collaboration policies and reproducibility infrastructure, making it in scope. It serves as a foundational overview of this initiative. The abstract also articulates high-level frameworks for quality control and domain distinctions, and its changelogs detail the iterative design and implementation process, including lessons learned from critiques.
institutions: Endometriosis UK; Three Rivers College; Open Knowledge (United Kingdom)
topics: Mathematics, Computing, and Information Processing; Academic Publishing and Open Access; Scientific Computing and Data Management

title: Managing Sustainable Data Infrastructures: The Gestalt of EOSDIS
authors: Jeanne Behnke, Dawn Lowe, Francis Lindsay, Christopher Lynnes, A. E. Mitchell
keywords: Metadata; Computer science; Component (thermodynamics); Data science; Stewardship (theology); Function (biology); Key (lock); Architecture; Cyberinfrastructure; Work (physics); Workflow; Systems engineering; Database; World Wide Web; Engineering; Computer security
abstract: EOSDIS epitomizes a System of Systems, whose many varied and distributed parts are integrated into a single, highly functional organized science data system. A distributed architecture was adopted to ensure discipline-specific support for the science data, while also leveraging standards and establishing policies and tools to enable interdisciplinary research, and analysis across multiple scientific instruments. The EOSDIS is composed of system elements such as geographically distributed archive centers used to manage the stewardship of data. The infrastructure consists of underlying capabilities connections that enable the primary system elements to function together. For example, one key infrastructure component is the common metadata repository, which enables discovery of all data within the EOSDIS system. EOSDIS employs processes and standards to ensure partners can work together effectively, and provide coherent services to users.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes the EOSDIS infrastructure, focusing on standards, policies, and metadata to enable interdisciplinary research. It serves as a descriptive overview of this specific, named data system. It does not propose generalized frameworks or diagnose challenges, but it does describe the design choices and implementation processes of EOSDIS, such as its distributed architecture and use of common metadata and standards.
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management

title: WDS/DSA Certification – International collaboration for a trustworthy research data infrastructure
authors: Mustapha Mokrane, Ingrid Dillo, Wim Hugo, Sandy P. Harrison
publisher: Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare, Sezione di Catania
keywords: Trustworthiness; Certification; Business; Computer security; Computer science; Political science; Law
abstract: Today’s research is international, transdisciplinary, and data-enabled, which requires scrupulous data stewardship, full and open access to data, and efficient collaboration and coordination. New expectations on researchers based on policies from governments and funders to share data fully, openly, and in a timely manner present significant challenges but are also opportunities to improve the quality and efficiency of research and its accountability to society. Researchers should be able to archive and disseminate data as required by many institutions or funders, and civil society to scrutinize datasets underlying public policies. Thus, the trustworthiness of data services must be verifiable. In addition, the need to integrate large and complex datasets across disciplines and domains with variable levels of maturity calls for greater coordination to achieve sufficient interoperability and sustainability. The World Data System (WDS) of the International Council for Science (ICSU) promotes long-term stewardship of, and universal and equitable access to, quality-assured scientific data and services across a range of disciplines in the natural and social sciences. WDS aims at coordinating and supporting trusted scientific data services for the provision, use, and preservation of relevant datasets to facilitate scientific research, in particular under the ICSU umbrella, while strengthening their links with the research community. WDS certifies its Members, holders and providers of data or data products, sing internationally recognized standards. Certification of scientific data services is essential to ensure trustworthiness of the global research data infrastructure. It contributes to building a searchable, distributed, interoperable and sustainable research data infrastructure. Several certification standards have been developed over the last decade, such as the Network of Expertise in long-term Storage and Accessibility of Digital Resources in Germany (NESTOR) seal, German Institute for Standardization (DIN) standard 31644, Trustworthy Repositories Audit and Certification (TRAC) criteria and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) standard 16363. In addition, the Data Seal of Approval (DSA) and WDS have set up core certification mechanisms for trusted digital repositories in 2009, which are increasingly recognized as de facto standards. While DSA emerged in Europe in the Humanities and Social Sciences, WDS started as an international initiative with historical roots in the Earth and Space Sciences. Their catalogues of requirements and review procedures are based on the same principles of openness, transparency. A unique feature of the DSA and WDS certification is that it strikes a balance between simplicity, robustness and the effort required to complete. A successful international cross-project collaboration was initiated between WDS and DSA under the umbrella of the Research Data Alliance (RDA), an international initiative started in 2013 to promote data interoperability which provided a useful and neutral forum. A joint working group was established in early 2014 to reconcile and simplify the array of certification options and improve and stimulate core certification for scientific data services. The outputs of this collaboration are a Catalogue of Common Requirements (https://goo.gl/LJZqDo) and a Catalogue of Common Procedures (https://goo.gl/vNR0q1) which will be implemented jointly by WDS and DSA.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses interdisciplinary collaboration for data stewardship and interoperability through certification standards. It provides an overview of the specific WDS/DSA certification collaboration and its outputs. It also addresses macroscopic challenges in data integration across disciplines and proposes a framework for trustworthy research data infrastructure through harmonized certification. Finally, it narrates the process of how the WDS and DSA collaborated to design and implement common certification requirements and procedures.
institutions: World Data System; South African Environmental Observation Network; University of Reading; Data Archiving Networked Services (DANS)
topics: Scientific Computing and Data Management; Research Data Management Practices

title: Community engagement in developing a sustainable Open Access data infrastructure for physical sciences
authors: Saul, Louise, Knight, Nicola, Pearman-Kanza, Samantha, Willoughby, Cerys
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Best practice; Standardization; Stewardship (theology); Open data; Data governance; Data quality; Data management; Set (abstract data type); Interoperability; Data access
abstract: As in many academic disciplines, challenges exist for researchers and research enablers in the physical sciences in ensuring that data and associated attributes are FAIR and published for re-use. Whilst the field is diverse and encapsulates many practices, the identified challenges include: data interoperability, loss of data when converting between different scientific data formats, heavy reliance on data generation through facilities, heterogeneity in naming, lack of uptake on tools to support best practice such as Electronic Lab Notebooks, and lack of training to support the required change in research culture. The Physical Sciences Data Infrastructure (PSDI) initiative is creating a data infrastructure that connects existing experimental and computational facilities within Physical Sciences and beyond to enhance research efficiency. PSDI looks to improve data handling across the research data lifecycle through the creation of tools and services that can be deployed within the researcher’s workflow, developed from community requirements, incorporating strategies for the skills & training, best practices and standardization activities needed alongside. Following an initial pilot and development phase, focusing on connecting existing infrastructures, data stewardship practices, and best use of people technology, PSDI has recently launched an initial set of resources for the physical sciences community in the form of services, software tools, data sources and guidance. PSDI enables researchers to use reference quality data from commercial and open sources; share resources within the community; make use of technologies such as AI to explore data; and learn how to make their research open and FAIR. Much of these services are available Open Access, however some are only Members-Only Open Access. In this session, we will talk about how PSDI developed workflows to promote community engagement and the activities undertaken in the initiative and discuss the process and challenges in making an Open Access Infrastructure.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it addresses FAIR data stewardship and interoperability challenges within the diverse physical sciences, including sociotechnical aspects. It serves as a definitive overview of the specific PSDI initiative. It also diagnoses macroscopic challenges hindering data interoperability and discusses the process and challenges of implementing the PSDI, making it a narrative of an implemented effort.
institutions: University of Southampton
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Scientific Computing and Data Management; Environmental Monitoring and Data Management

title: Geolinking Service SoRa - A FAIR Social-Spatial Science Research Data Infrastructure
authors: Zapilko, Benjamin, Goebel, Jan, Jünger, Stefan, Jung, Alexander, Lieth, Jonas, Meinel, Gotthard, Rieche, Theodor, Sikder, Sujit Kumar
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Workflow; Geocoding; Process (computing); Service (business); Key (lock); Interface (matter); Big data; Spatial data infrastructure
abstract: The integration of social and spatial data offers significant potential for interdisciplinary research, but it is constrained by infrastructural, legal, and methodological challenges. The Geolinking Service SoRa addresses these by providing a sustainable, privacy-compliant research data infrastructure that enables the linkage of social science survey data with georeferenced spatial information. Building on established platforms such as the IOER-Monitor, ALLBUS, and SOEP, the Geolinking Service SoRa supports complex analyses at the interface of the social and spatial sciences. A key innovation of SoRa is supporting the entire analysis process that involves geocoded survey data, including data preparation, linkage, and analysis within a secure environment. The infrastructure includes a user module, a public/private mode API, and a Geolinking API, along with R and Stata packages to support efficient workflows. Several geolinking methods are implemented, including point-to-point, buffer, isochrone-based, and routing to the nearest point of interest (e.g., schools or hospitals). Additionally, SoRa facilitates the preparation of analyses using structural datasets that mimic spatial distributions without exposing sensitive data and compromising data privacy. These synthetic datasets are tailored to specific survey datasets, it can enable researchers to test workflows and conduct preliminary, explorative analyses before accessing secure room environments. This poster presents the project’s progress, from defining infrastructure components to creating prototypes and implementing privacy-preserving features. A live demonstration will showcase how SoRa can be used to enhance social-spatial research applications. By promoting FAIR and inclusive data integration, SoRa lays a strong foundation for interdisciplinary research, empowering scholars to tackle innovative anddata-driven questions with confidence.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes an interdisciplinary research data infrastructure (SoRa) for social and spatial sciences, focusing on FAIR principles and data linkage. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific initiative. It does not primarily focus on macroscopic frameworks or a generalized diagnosis of challenges, but rather on a specific solution. However, it does tell the story of how SoRa was designed and implemented by detailing the project's progress, from defining components to creating prototypes and implementing features.
institutions: German Institute for Economic Research; GESIS - Leibniz-Institute for the Social Sciences; Leibniz Institute of Ecological Urban and Regional Development
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Spatial and Panel Data Analysis; Data-Driven Disease Surveillance

title: Hybrid collectives for effective spatial data infrastructure development in the global south : developing a Zambian prototype.
authors: Levi Shimi Mutambo
source: University of Canterbury Research Repository (University of Canterbury)
keywords: Computer science; Developing country; Geography; Economic growth
abstract: The aim of this thesis was to generate a theoretical framework to help people understand how to better develop spatial data infrastructures (SDIs) in the Global South that could lead to addressing the challenges of access to location information for grassroots communities. This resulted in the deployment and extension of the hybrid collective and commons concept in SDI development. Current approaches to SDI development have neglected the grassroots and their location information needs as well as their potential to contribute to the SDI digital-knowledge resource, a shortcoming this thesis addresses by introducing what I term the “hybrid SDI collective” framework. I asked, “How can we understand the socio-technical infrastructure of SDI development in such a way as to ensure that SDIs in the Global South may be created and maintained?” I did so to enable the GIScience community to understand SDI development as a hybrid collective activity of laypeople, professionals, and non-human actants. Using a case study of smallholder farmers in rural Zambia involving semi-structured interviews, focus group discussions, using the Agile software development approach to develop a mobile GIS app, and field testing, I showed that an SDI can be developed from free resources and managed as a commons by a community made up of both experts and laypeople located in the Global South. I also showed that laypeople have the potential to make valuable contributions to digital location information and to the management of SDIs through their experience in managing commons. The significance of this study is that it informs our theoretical understanding of SDI development by focusing on resource-constrained and marginalised communities hitherto ignored. It thus informs our empirical understanding of participatory approaches to smartphone-based VGI.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it proposes a theoretical framework for developing spatial data infrastructures (SDIs) as a 'hybrid collective' managed as a 'commons' by experts and laypeople, addressing sociotechnical challenges of data stewardship across distinct groups. It does not primarily serve as an overview of an existing initiative, but rather introduces a macroscopic framework for how SDIs should be implemented and diagnoses related challenges. Furthermore, it evaluates the implementation process of a prototype SDI through a case study, extracting lessons learned about involving diverse communities in data management.
topics: ICT in Developing Communities

title: [Thesis defense] Data infrastructures and knowledge governance in participatory science: An inquiry into practices and framework development
authors: Karen Soacha Godoy
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Framing (construction); Corporate governance; Participatory GIS; Citizen journalism; Data governance; Participatory action research; Commons; Deliberation; General partnership
abstract: This slide deck supports the public defense of the PhD thesis Data infrastructures and knowledge governance in participatory science: An inquiry into practices and framework development, Doctoral Program on Information and Knowledge Society, Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC), Barcelona, Spain. The defense took place on 23 February 2026 at ICM–CSIC. The presentation argues that digital infrastructures in participatory science (citizen science platforms and citizen observatories) do more than support data collection and sharing. Their technical and organisational configurations shape who can contribute, who can access and reuse outputs, and who is accountable when data travel across contexts. The talk develops this argument through an integrated conceptual framing that connects infrastructure studies (including relational and practice based views of infrastructure), knowledge infrastructure perspectives, knowledge commons scholarship, and research on citizen science participation, while also engaging equity oriented approaches to data governance. The empirical core synthesises a structured mapping of the literature and an infrastructure focused analysis of platform features and arrangements. The results highlight five claims: platforms do not have a single governance identity; governance roles shift across arenas; design choices encode governance decisions; open science does not imply uniform governance; and platforms actively distribute power across contributors, institutions, and downstream users. A key contribution presented is the SEXTANT Governance Model, introduced as a diagnostic model to examine governance across three action arenas (Ownership, Sharing, and Accountability) and to characterise how platforms adopt different role orientations in practice (for example, enabling, mediating, or gatekeeping). The final section summarises the thesis contributions for research and practice, discusses limitations and scope conditions, and outlines future directions for building more transparent, contestable, and equitable participatory infrastructures. A video recording of the thesis defense is available on YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/live/ZUy36h_Y_zE
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on knowledge governance and data infrastructures in participatory science, addressing interdisciplinary challenges in data stewardship. It does not primarily introduce a specific initiative but rather proposes the SEXTANT Governance Model as a macroscopic framework for diagnosing structural challenges in how these infrastructures are governed. The paper also conducts an inquiry into practices and an infrastructure-focused analysis, aligning with a narrative evaluation of how governance is implemented.
institutions: Institut Català de Ciències del Clima
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Digital Education and Society; Information Systems Theories and Implementation

title: Shared Data, Shared Practice, Shared Knowledge. Insights from Building NFDI4Culture's Federated Research Data Infrastructure for Cultural Heritage
authors: Schrade, Torsten, Söhn, Linnaea Charlotte, Büttner, Alexandra, Tietz, Tabea, Steller, Jonatan Jalle, Bruns, Oleksandra, Posthumus, Etienne, Fliegl, Heike, Sack, Harald

abstract: These slides were presented at the Digital Heritage International Congress 2025 in Siena, Italy (8.-13. September 2025) in "Track 03 – Infrastructures, Dataspace, International Projects". NFDI4Culture is a consortium within Germany's National Research Data Infrastructure (NFDI), dedicated to the systematic management, preservation, and reuse of research data on tangible and intangible cultural heritage. Emphasising a federated approach and comprehensive implementation of the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable), NFDI4Culture fosters interdisciplinary collaboration, enhances research opportunities, and connects diverse cultural heritage data from fields such as architecture, art history, musicology, performing arts, and media studies.

The title "Insights from Building NFDI4Culture's..." indicates it tells the story of how this specific infrastructure was designed and implemented.

title: Interoperability in an Infrastructure Enabling Multidisciplinary Research: The case of CLARIN
authors: Franciska de Jong, Bente Mægaard, Darja Fišer, Dieter Van Uytvanck, Andreas Witt
source: Publication Server of the Institute for German Language (Institute for German Language)
publisher: Leibniz Institute for the German Language
keywords: Interoperability; Multidisciplinary approach; Workflow; Computer science; Data science; Cloud computing; Grand Challenges; Knowledge management; World Wide Web; Political science; Database
abstract: CLARIN is a European Research Infrastructure providing access to language resources and technologies for researchers in the humanities and social sciences. It supports the use and study of language data in general and aims to increase the potential for comparative research of cultural and societal phenomena across the boundaries of languages and disciplines, all in line with the European agenda for Open Science. Data infrastructures such as CLARIN have recently embarked on the emerging frameworks for the federation of infrastructural services, such as the European Open Science Cloud and the integration of services resulting from multidisciplinary collaboration in federated services for the wider domain of the social sciences and humanities (SSH). In this paper we describe the interoperability requirements that arise through the existing ambitions and the emerging frameworks. The interoperability theme will be addressed at several levels, including organisation and ecosystem, design of workflow services, data curation, performance measurement and collaboration. For each level, some concrete outcomes are described.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses interdisciplinary interoperability requirements for research data within the CLARIN infrastructure, spanning humanities and social sciences. It serves as a descriptive overview of CLARIN's interoperability efforts. It also addresses macroscopic interoperability requirements and outcomes at organizational and ecosystem levels, linking to broader frameworks like EOSC. Finally, it describes how interoperability is addressed within CLARIN at various levels, providing concrete outcomes that reflect the implementation process.

title: Highlights of the 2nd Interdisciplinary NFDI Metadata Workshop
authors: Leyla Jael Castro, Andreas Czerniak, Heike Fliegl, Christin Henzen, Oliver Koepler, Josh Moore, Emanuel Söding, Ulrik Stervbo, Thorsten Trippel, Cord Wiljes
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Metadata; Interoperability; Meta Data Services; Geospatial metadata; Metadata repository; Process (computing); Data management plan; Discipline
abstract: The German National Research Data Infrastructure (NFDI) unifies activities across all scientific disciplines, represented by NFDI consortia, to realise an overarching research data management. Interdisciplinary domain analysis and agreement on common metadata standards for the description of research outputs are key elements for a successful FAIR implementation. The Taskforce Metadata within the NFDI Section (Meta)data, Terminologies, Provenance coordinates the community process of assessing existing cross-disciplinary terminologies and metadata schemas as suitable candidates for NFDI-agreed recommendations. A first workshop was held in Dresden in January 2025 to begin alignment on metadata [1]. Here, we present the results of the second interactive workshop organized by the Taskforce at the TIB Leibniz Information Centre for Science and Technology in June 2025. The goal was to identify and evaluate suitable existing generic metadata schemas and provide recommendations on how to apply these schemas in the individual disciplines. Altogether, 61 participants from 25 NFDI consortia discussed common metadata schemas, disciplinary needs, and standardisation approaches. The recommendation from the first workshop supporting the registration of repositories in re3data was reaffirmed. Thus, the consensus is now being translated into the official NFDI recommendation document. Additionally, breakout sessions facilitated cross-disciplinary discussions on using the metadata schemas identified in the first workshop – DCAT-AP, DataCite and schema.org – and how to connect these to disciplinary schemas. The Taskforce Metadata recommends that NFDI consortia adopt at least one of these three standards, thereby advancing interoperability on an international level, e.g. in the EOSC context. [1] A. Czerniak, L. J. Castro, H. Fliegl, J. Grieb, C. Henzen, O. Koepler, J. Moore, E. Söding, U. Stervbo, T. Trippel, C. Wiljes, Building Bridges through Metadata within the NFDI, https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17195542 and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.16735955
rationale: The paper is in scope as it details an interdisciplinary workshop focused on achieving consensus on common metadata standards and schemas for FAIR data stewardship across diverse scientific disciplines within the NFDI. It serves as an overview of this specific initiative and its recommendations for broad implementation. The paper also describes the process and outcomes of this consensus-building effort, providing an experience report of how standards were discussed and recommended.
