title: FAIR assessment tools as supporters for interdisciplinary research?
authors: Anna Lehmann-Gnirck
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Operationalization; Metadata; Context (archaeology); Quality (philosophy); Bridging (networking); Situated; Consistency (knowledge bases); Rubric
abstract: Metadata recommendations only unfold their effect when translated into concrete research practice. Within the context of the FAIR principles (Wilkinson et al. 2016), FAIR assessment tools increasingly assume this translational role by interpreting metadata into measurable indicators for FAIRness. They are frequently understood as neutral instruments for assessing data quality (Pellegrino & Tuozzo 2025). However, initial studies from infrastructure and community contexts suggest that FAIR is operationalised differently across existing assessment metrics, leading to inconsistent assessment outcomes (Gehlen et. al 2022; Devaraju & Huber 2021). These inconsistencies are mainly expressions of various communities of practice in which FAIR becomes actionable. The poster conceptualises FAIR assessment tools as analytical translation mechanisms situated between discipline-specific data practices and technical information infrastructure (Wilkinson et al. 2016). The key concern is on how these tools can be used across disciplinary boundaries to support heterogeneous data aggregation, bridging diverse (meta-)data practices. Based on an understanding of FAIR as a domain-independent information model, this contribution examines how individual assessment tools interpret and operationalize its principles differently. Methodologically, this study draws on a comparative analysis of selected FAIR assessment tools (including FUJ-I, FAIR Assessment Tool by TKFDM, ACME-Fair) along with systematically developed criteria related to 15 principles and to 41 indicators (RDA FAIR Data Maturity WG 2020, p. 6) to evaluate these tools. To make the tools’ implicit requirements explicit, a reference dataset is outlined as a representation of key features to emphasize aspects for analysis. The heuristic guides dataset selection for subsequent comparative tool analysis. The poster contributes to the discussion by showing how a transparent and reflective use of assessment metrics can help to frame metadata not merely as an unpopular requirement, but as a configurable means for supporting interdisciplinary data aggregation.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on FAIR assessment tools as mechanisms for bridging diverse (meta-)data practices across disciplinary boundaries. It does not introduce a new initiative but rather conducts a comparative analysis of existing FAIR assessment tools. The paper diagnoses the structural challenge of inconsistent FAIR operationalization across communities and proposes a conceptual understanding of these tools as "analytical translation mechanisms." This comparative analysis serves as an evaluation of how these tools interpret and operationalize FAIR principles, providing insights into the process of achieving interdisciplinary data aggregation.
institutions: University of Applied Sciences Potsdam
topics: Research Data Management Practices; Library Science and Information Systems; scientometrics and bibliometrics research

title: Open Data Repository for multidisciplinary scientific community: Bridge of Data repository project developed at Gdańsk University of Technology
authors: Aleksander Mroziński, Magdalena Szuflita‐Żurawska, Anna Wałek, Krajewski, Piotr, Robert Szczodruch
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Bridge (graph theory); Multidisciplinary approach; Engineering; Engineering management; Computer science; Data science; Sociology; Medicine; Social science
abstract: Currently, Open Data Repositories are being increasingly used due to the emerging importance of open science and the requirements of grant funding agencies. Despite growing interest, it is challenging to find a repository with a universal standard for metadata description for many fields of science. This paper presents the motivation to identify a universal interdisciplinary metadata description scheme for research data based on the needs of the three major Pomeranian universities (the Gdańsk University of Technology, the Medical University of Gdańsk and the University of Gdańsk) and creating a support infrastructure with a wide range of services – the Open Science Competence Center. The presented experiences may be helpful for other institutions that plan to create an Open Data Repository to avoid re-analysis of already-researched problems, especially if they represent many scientific disciplines such as universities or technical universities. The results of the successful cooperation between universities and their different stakeholders (e.g. librarians, IT specialists, and researchers) show that there are eleven metadata attributes common to many scientific disciplines. Based on the findings, Gdańsk University of Technology was able to develop an institutional Open Data Repository under the Bridge of Data project. The process of implementing the Bridge of Data Repository and its main functionalities is discussed in detail.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses the development of an interdisciplinary metadata description scheme and support infrastructure for research data across multiple universities. It serves as a definitive overview of the specific 'Bridge of Data' repository project. However, it does not propose macroscopic frameworks or generalized diagnoses of challenges. Instead, it narrates the process of implementing this specific repository and shares lessons learned from its development.
institutions: Gdańsk University of Technology
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies

title: Metadata Management in an Inter Disciplinary
authors: Uzair Shaikh, Akanksha Kulkarni
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Metadata; Discipline; Computer science; Meta Data Services; World Wide Web; Information retrieval; Data science; Database; Metadata repository; Sociology; Social science
abstract: Data Assimilation’. In this framework, a data repository, the so-called TR32DB project database, was established in 2008 with the aim to manage the resulting data of the involved scientists [1]. The data documentation with accurate, extensive metadata has been a key task. Consequently, a standardized, interoperable, multi-level metadata schema has been designed and implemented to ensure a proper documentation and publication of all project data (e.g. data, publication, reports), as well as to facilitate data search, exchange and -use. A user-friendly web-interface was designed for a simple metadata input and search. As well as major cultural and organizational changes in the external operating environment, from which a sustainable business model for digital preservation has emerged. This article will take a retrospective look at challenges that have been faced and will review current and future priorities for those seeking to establish digital repositories.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it discusses interdisciplinary metadata management and an interoperable schema for research data, addressing sociotechnical challenges. It is not a definitive overview of a specific initiative, but rather takes a retrospective look at challenges and reviews priorities for establishing digital repositories in general. It diagnoses structural challenges related to cultural/organizational changes and sustainable business models. Finally, it serves as an experience report by reflecting on challenges faced during the implementation of the TR32DB project.
topics: Library Science and Information Systems

title: Data Harmonization Report. AtlantOS project
authors: Koop-Jakobsen,, Christoph Waldmann, G. Hüber, Valérie Harscoat, Sylvie Pouliquen
source: Institutional Archive of Ifremer (French Research Institute for Exploitation of the Sea)
keywords: Discoverability; Harmonization; Data sharing; Interoperability; Computer science; Metadata; Data quality; Context (archaeology); Data management; Process management; Data science; World Wide Web; Business; Database; Geography
abstract: AtlantOS WP7 is dedicated to improve harmonization of data management procedures, and thereby improve the quality, interoperability and discoverability of data resources in AtlantOS. To improve harmonization, AtlantOS WP7 works on multiple levels; a) WP7 has identified selected areas, where significant improvements of interoperability can be obtained. This has resulted in the formulation of a common agreement stating a set of specific minimum standards, which shall ensure cross platform coherence. This includes minimum standards for use of identifiers for platforms and institutions, metadata including vocabularies, quality control and dissemination means. Furthermore, guidelines regarding DOI assignment, catalogue techniques and vocabulary use in AtlantOS have been formulated. b) AtlantOS has formulated and installed a Data Management Plan (DMP) setting the framework for handling and dissemination of AtlantOS data. This was the first step towards improved harmonization and includes an overview of the Data Landscape, prioritization of Essential Variables for AtlantOS, regulations regarding open access to data and recommendations on use of standards. c) AtlantOS WP7 is initiating investigations of the use of GEOSS services, both for technical broker solutions to improve harmonization as well as for dissemination of AtlantOS data resources in an interdisciplinary global context. d) AtlantOS is also working on improving the transcontinental data sharing. A workshop is planned for in 2017 specifically targeting improvement of transcontinental sharing of data from the Atlantic Ocean. We here present the preliminary incentives for improving the transatlantic collaboration.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on interdisciplinary data harmonization, metadata, vocabularies, and standards within the AtlantOS project, addressing interoperability challenges across different platforms and institutions. It serves as a descriptive overview of the specific AtlantOS WP7 initiative and its efforts. However, it does not propose macroscopic, generalized frameworks or diagnose systemic challenges beyond its specific project. It does tell the story of how the AtlantOS project is designing and implementing its data harmonization efforts, detailing the steps taken and standards formulated.
topics: Environmental Monitoring and Data Management; Research Data Management Practices; Oceanographic and Atmospheric Processes

title: D8.2 Overview Document on Already Existing Data Harmonisation Tools
authors: Sebastian Mate, Ines Leb, Christina Schüttler, Kaisa Silander, Timo Miettinen, Juha Knuuttila, Niina Eklund, Hans‐Ulrich Prokosch
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Computer science; Data science; Information retrieval; World Wide Web
abstract: The aim of this document is to analyse the state of the art and existing tools for data harmonisation, considering their applicability within CS-IT. In addition, it will include information on the different levels and versions of various standardised and non-standardised ontologies and data dictionaries and schemas. The major focus of this document is to present some examples of already ongoing harmonisation projects in the field of biobanking and biological sample collections, as well as aiming to support the project by providing a literature review on the biobanks' needs regarding the harmonisation tools and processes described in BBMRI-ERIC CS-IT's Requirements Specification document (Eklund 2016). Since data harmonisation, however, is not only an important issue in biobanking, but a key component in all data sharing/data projects, our focus is also on such projects outside the biobanking area. This review has been done as a deliverable of the Biobanking and Biomolecular Resources Research Infrastructure – European Research Infrastructure Consortium (BBMRI-ERIC) Common Service IT (CS-IT) as well as the Horizon2020 INFRADEV project ADOPT. The current version is based on a comprehensive literature review and experiences from own work in this field as well as based on communication with colleagues in related interdisciplinary multi-partner research projects. This work is part of the ADOPT BBMRI-ERIC project, funded by the European Commission, topic H2020- INFRADEV-3-2015, Grant Agreement Nr 676550.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on interdisciplinary data harmonisation, ontologies, and schemas across biobanking and other domains. It is not an overview of a specific new initiative, but rather a landscape analysis of existing tools and needs, which serves as a macroscopic diagnosis of the state of the art. Furthermore, it is a literature review of ongoing harmonisation projects and tools, fitting the criteria for a systematic review of such efforts.
institutions: Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare; Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland; Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg
topics: Machine Learning and Data Classification; Data Mining Algorithms and Applications

title: Methodology for CIDOC CRM based data integration with spatial data
authors: Gerald Hiebel, K. Hanke, Ingrid Hayek
keywords: Computer science; Information retrieval; Data science; Process (computing); Scope (computer science); Data integration; Data model (GIS); Database; World Wide Web; Artificial intelligence
abstract: In this paper we want to present a methodology for data integration based on the CIDOC CRM. Spatial data are included in the integration process which provides us on the one hand with the possibility to access the CRM structured data through an interactive map. On the other hand in future GIS functionalities of spatial analysis can generate new data within the ontological database that could not be generated with inference mechanisms. Our approach emphasizes the scalability of the ontological data integration process. It gives the user the possibility to do a gradual transfer to ontological structures depending on the needs and available resources. Thus it should be easier to adopt an ontological data model. The methodology is divided into a conceptual part and an implementation part. There are three steps of conceptual work that range from a scope definition over CRM class and properties identification to a thesaurus specification. The example of an excavation illustrates how the newly build conceptual model of CRM classes and thesaurus can represent a real world situation. The implementation work is divided into four steps that lead from the creation of a web repository for digital resources and a relational database with user and GIS interface to a RDF/OWL representation of the data that can be further processed with semantic tools. The methodology was developed in the course of the multidisciplinary project HiMAT where eleven disciplines research commonly on the history of mining activities from prehistoric to modern times. Every methodological step is exemplified with the way it was realized within the project HiMAT.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it presents a methodology for interdisciplinary data integration using CIDOC CRM and ontological structures within a multidisciplinary project. It is not an overview of a specific data commons but rather proposes a generalized methodology (framework) for semantic harmonization. The paper also describes how this methodology was realized within the HiMAT project, providing an account of its implementation.
institutions: Universität Innsbruck
topics: Geographic Information Systems Studies; Semantic Web and Ontologies; Data Management and Algorithms

title: Generative AI-augmented and User-centric Research Data Discovery and Reuse
authors: Lizhou Fan
keywords: Reuse; Computer science; Generative grammar; Human–computer interaction; World Wide Web; Information retrieval; Artificial intelligence; Engineering
abstract: This dissertation addresses the challenge of enhancing research data discovery and reuse in the face of escalating data volume and complexity. Traditional metadata-driven search tools often fall short in providing nuanced context and interdisciplinary connections critical for efficient scientific exploration and collaboration. To address these limitations, we developed the Generative AI-augmented and User-centric Data Search (GAUDS) system, which integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) and Scholarly Knowledge Graphs (SKGs) to parse natural language queries and visualize data relationships, thereby fostering a deeper understanding of available research resources. The study details the development and implementation of the GAUDS system, including the conceptualization of the guiding principles Connectivity, Effectiveness, Visibility and Interactivity (CEVI) that support and evaluate the discovery and reuse of research data. It further explores the construction of the ICPSR Health and Medical Scholarly Knowledge Graph (IHSKG), which represents complex connections in research data and prototypes interdisciplinary reuse potentials. The abilities of LLMs to perform complex reasoning were assessed, informing the system's ability to understand and manipulate large datasets effectively. The development of the GAUDS system, informed by insights gained from prototyping and evaluating user-centric utility, leads to a comprehensive analysis of focus group feedback. This feedback evaluates the system’s impact on enhancing data discoverability and usability. The GAUDS system, by providing effective navigation aids, relevant dataset suggestions, and contextualized reuse guides, not only enhances user engagement and satisfaction, but also demonstrates the transformative potential of generative AI in specialized academic domains such as health and medical research. This research contributes to the fields of information retrieval and data management by proposing a novel approach that combines human-curated knowledge graphs with generative AI algorithms to significantly improve data discovery and reuse. Future work will aim to productionize the GAUDS system, expand its scalability across different domains, and explore its broader potential to support open science initiatives.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes the development of the GAUDS system to enhance interdisciplinary research data discovery and reuse, focusing on semantic interoperability and metadata. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific initiative (GAUDS system and IHSKG). It does not propose macroscopic frameworks for the field, but rather principles specific to the system's design. The paper evaluates the development and implementation of the GAUDS system, including user-centric utility and focus group feedback, aligning with an experience report.
topics: Data Mining Algorithms and Applications; Semantic Web and Ontologies; Data Quality and Management

title: Developing indonesian medicinal plant ontology using socio-technical approach
authors: Mesnan Silalahi, Denis Eka Cahyani, Dana Indra Sensuse, Indra Budi
keywords: Indonesian; Ontology; Computer science; Data science; Information retrieval
abstract: Data and information about Indonesian genetic resources and traditional knowledge, especially the current data on medicinal plants is not managed optimally yet. The data and information is still scattered which if there is no integrated management action done, the data and information might be lost. One effort to integrate the data about medicinal plants is by building the ontology. This research develops a socio-technical model in ontology engineering by taking a case study of the development of Indonesian medicinal plants ontology. The ontology will support the development of applications based on Indonesian medicinal plants. Sources of data in this ontology considered from five aspects such as pharmacology, health practitioners, plant taxonomy, plant cultivation, and conservation of rare medicinal plants. The results of this study initiate the establishment of Indonesian medicinal plants ontology that is needed by various parties and support protection and utilization of genetic resources in Indonesia optimally. Validation of the ontology is carried out through a functionality of list data of competency questions executed on a semantic web application.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes the development of an interdisciplinary ontology for Indonesian medicinal plants, focusing on semantic interoperability across diverse scientific perspectives using a socio-technical approach. It serves as an overview of this specific ontology development effort. It also tells the story of how this specific ontology is being designed and implemented through a socio-technical model, considering various disciplinary aspects. However, it does not propose macroscopic frameworks or diagnose challenges in a generalized, structural way, but rather focuses on a specific case study.
institutions: University of Indonesia
topics: Ethnobotanical and Medicinal Plants Studies; Agricultural and Biological Research; Coconut Research and Applications

title: A case study in a socio-technical approach in ontology engineering
authors: Mesnan Silalahi, Dana Indra Sensuse
keywords: Ontology; Ontology engineering; Computer science; Process ontology; Ontology-based data integration; Upper ontology; Domain (mathematical analysis); Suggested Upper Merged Ontology; Point (geometry); Knowledge management; Data science; Software engineering; Domain knowledge; Mathematics; Epistemology
abstract: This paper describes results from the study case in developing an ontology for the Indonesian medicinal plants. The approach to the new way of developing ontology is taken from the point view of a socio-technical system theory. The case study was conducted after the theoretical model development developed through a qualitative research synthesis which aimed as a proof of concept. We describe a socio-technical ontology engineering approach for developing an ontology. The approach was applied within all of the phases in the life-cycle of the ontology and involved about ten domain experts from research centers and universities. The results show a promising way of conducting ontology engineering to ensure success.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes a socio-technical approach to ontology engineering for scientific data (medicinal plants) involving multiple domain experts, addressing semantic interoperability. It does not primarily introduce a specific named initiative. However, it proposes a generalized socio-technical framework for ontology development and serves as a case study evaluating the process of implementing this approach, extracting lessons learned about successful ontology engineering.
institutions: University of Indonesia; Indonesian Institute of Sciences
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies; Service-Oriented Architecture and Web Services; Web Applications and Data Management

title: On Supporting A Meta-Design Approach In Socio-Technical Ontology Engineering
authors: Mesnan Silalahi, Dana Indra Sensuse, Indra Budi
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Ontology; Computer science; Metamodeling; Software engineering; Ontology engineering; Systems engineering; Process ontology; Engineering; Epistemology
abstract:
Many studies have revealed the fact of the complexity
of ontology building process. Therefore there is a need for a new
approach which one of that addresses the socio-technical aspects in the
collaboration to reach a consensus. Meta-design approach is
considered applicable as a method in the methodological model of
socio-technical ontology engineering. Principles in the meta-design
framework are applied in the construction phases of the ontology. A
web portal is developed to support the meta-design principles
requirements. To validate the methodological model semantic web
applications were developed and integrated in the portal and also used
as a way to show the usefulness of the ontology. The knowledge based
system will be filled with data of Indonesian medicinal plants. By
showing the usefulness of the developed ontology in a semantic web
application, we motivate all stakeholders to participate in the
development of knowledge based system of medicinal plants in
Indonesia.
rationale: The paper discusses a socio-technical meta-design approach for ontology engineering to achieve consensus among stakeholders for a knowledge-based system of medicinal plants, aligning with interdisciplinary FAIR data stewardship. Its primary focus is on the meta-design approach and methodological model for ontology engineering, not a definitive overview of a specific, named data commons. It proposes a generalized "meta-design approach" as a framework for socio-technical ontology engineering. It also describes the process of applying this framework, developing a portal, and validating the methodological model, detailing how an ontology and supporting system were designed and implemented.
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies

title: An Ontology Design Pattern of the Multidisciplinary and Complex Field of Climate Change
authors: Ibrahima Diop, Moussa Lô
keywords: Ontology; Multidisciplinary approach; Process ontology; Upper ontology; Ontology-based data integration; Suggested Upper Merged Ontology; Field (mathematics); Computer science; Domain (mathematical analysis); Climate change; Data science; Domain knowledge; Software engineering; Ecology; Mathematics; Political science
abstract: This article presents the manual and collaborative construction of an ontology design pattern (a generic ontology), named OntoCLUVA, of climate change (CC) field. This pattern is built for the needs of the construction of climate change ontologies. We used this pattern for a knowledge management system (KMS) of climate change. It will allow to each module of this KMS to build its own ontology of climate change domain for its tasks consisting in discovering and adapting the ontology components.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes the collaborative construction of an ontology design pattern (OntoCLUVA) for the multidisciplinary field of climate change, focusing on semantic interoperability. It serves as an overview of this specific initiative. The ontology design pattern also functions as a generalized framework for building climate change ontologies. Furthermore, it describes the 'manual and collaborative construction' and use of this pattern, detailing how it was designed and implemented.
institutions: Université Gaston Berger
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies; Service-Oriented Architecture and Web Services; Biomedical Text Mining and Ontologies

title: Representation of behaviour change interventions and their evaluation: Development of the Upper Level of the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology [version 1; peer review: awaiting peer review]
authors: Susan Michie, Robert West, AN Finnerty, Emma Norris, Amika S. Wright, Marta M. Marques, Marjorie C. Johnston, Michael Kelly, James Thomas, Justine Hastings
keywords: Ontology; Psychological intervention; Computer science; Context (archaeology); Intervention (counseling); Upper ontology; Knowledge management; Psychology; Domain knowledge
abstract: BACKGROUND: Behaviour change interventions (BCI), their contexts and evaluation methods are heterogeneous, making it difficult to synthesise evidence and make recommendations for real-world policy and practice. Ontologies provide a means for addressing this. They represent knowledge formally as entities and relationships using a common language able to cross disciplinary boundaries and topic domains. This paper reports the development of the upper level of the Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology (BCIO), which provides a systematic way to characterise BCIs, their contexts and their evaluations. METHODS: Development took place in four steps. (1) Entities and relationships were identified by behavioural and social science experts, based on their knowledge of evidence and theory, and their practical experience of behaviour change interventions and evaluations. (2) The outputs of the first step were critically examined by a wider group of experts, including the study ontology expert and those experienced in annotating relevant literature using the initial ontology entities. The outputs of the second step were tested by (3) feedback from three external international experts in ontologies and (4) application of the prototype upper-level BCIO to annotating published reports; this informed the final development of the upper-level BCIO. RESULTS: The final upper-level BCIO specifies 42 entities, including the BCI scenario, elaborated across 21 entities and 7 relationship types, and the BCI evaluation study comprising 10 entities and 9 relationship types. BCI scenario entities include the behaviour change intervention (content and delivery), outcome behaviour, mechanism of action, and its context, which includes population and setting. These entities have corresponding entities relating to the planning and reporting of interventions and their evaluations. CONCLUSIONS: The upper level of the BCIO provides a comprehensive and systematic framework for representing BCIs, their contexts and their evaluations.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it describes the development of an ontology (BCIO) to enable semantic interoperability across disciplinary boundaries for behaviour change intervention data. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific ontology. However, it does not propose a macroscopic or domain-agnostic framework for data stewardship. It does provide a narrative of how the BCIO was designed and implemented through a multi-step, expert-driven process.
institutions: University College London; University of Aberdeen; Trinity College Dublin; University of Cambridge
topics: Community Health and Development; Health Policy Implementation Science; Behavioral Health and Interventions

title: Agentic AI Ontology
authors: Paola Di Maio
keywords: Interoperability; Ontology; Semantic interoperability; Upper ontology; Schema (genetic algorithms); Semantic Web; Ontology-based data integration; Suggested Upper Merged Ontology
abstract: Agentic AI systems — computational entities that perceive, reason, and act with varying degrees ofautonomy — are rapidly proliferating across digital, physical, and socio-technical environments.Despite decades of research in intelligent agents, multi-agent systems, cognitive architectures, andhuman–AI interaction, there is no widely adopted cross-domain semantic framework that supportsconsistent description, comparison, and interoperability of agentic systems. This paper introducesversion 1.0 of an ontology for Agentic AI designed as a unifying semantic layer across thesetraditions. The ontology is structured along six primary dimensions: autonomy level, functional role,embodiment, cognitive capability, interaction style, and operational scope. Across thesedimensions, it defines 25 core agent classes and aligns them with relevant web and semanticstandards, including RDF, OWL, SPARQL, and related interoperability technologies. Rather thanproposing entirely new categories, the framework synthesizes established classification schemesfrom the literature and organizes them within a multi-dimensional model suitable for formalrepresentation. We describe the literature analysis, design methodology, ontology structure, andevaluation criteria, and provide machine-readable artifacts in both JSON Schema and OWL tosupport reuse, extension, and integration with knowledge graphs and web-based systems. Theontology is positioned as shared semantic infrastructure intended to reduce conceptualfragmentation, support interoperability, and facilitate the development of open, evolvableecosystems of agentic AI systems.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it introduces a cross-domain ontology for Agentic AI to achieve semantic interoperability across distinct research traditions. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific ontology, which acts as a macroscopic framework to address conceptual fragmentation. Furthermore, it describes the design methodology and evaluation criteria, detailing how this semantic infrastructure was developed.
topics: Embodied and Extended Cognition; Semantic Web and Ontologies; Language and cultural evolution

title: Development of an ontology for biofilms
authors: Thiruvarangan Ramaraj, Bo Wen Liu, Britney Gibbs, Azalea C. Mendoza, David L. Millman, Brendan Mumey, Matthew W. Fields, Callum J. Bell
keywords: Ontology; Computer science; Biofilm; Information retrieval; Bacteria; Geology; Epistemology
abstract: Microorganisms make up most of the earth’s biomass, and most microbes exist in the form of biofilms, complex communities of microorganisms growing attached to surfaces. Biofilms are directly relevant to a large number of scientific disciplines, and are the subjects of growing multidisciplinary research. As such, there is a pressing requirement for information systems that specialize in biofilm knowledge. Realization of such systems will require a coherent approach to understanding and curating the language used to study biofilms; an ontology of biofilms-related terms offers a foundation for such systems. Here we present an ontology for the study of biofilms (BIFO), a tool that will provide precisely defined terms describing all aspects involved in the biofilms domain. We describe semi-automated methods for the identification of relevant terms from a body of literature, the selection of a set of important terms by domain experts, and the construction of the ontology. A generic approach for BIFO is presented, in which foundational biofilm-related entities and relationships are represented. This ontology reuses terms from other ontologies that provide biofilm knowledge from the Open Biological and Biomedical Ontologies (OBO) foundry.
rationale: The paper is in scope (Q1) because it describes the development of an ontology (BIFO) for the multidisciplinary field of biofilms, focusing on semantic interoperability. It serves as a definitive overview of this specific ontology (Q2). It does not propose macroscopic frameworks or diagnose systemic challenges beyond the specific domain (Q3). However, it does detail the methods and process of how the ontology was constructed, including the involvement of domain experts, thus telling the story of its implementation (Q4).
institutions: Montana State University; National Center for Genome Resources; DePaul University
topics: Biomedical Text Mining and Ontologies; Bioinformatics and Genomic Networks; Microbial Community Ecology and Physiology

title: Untangling Scenario Terminology to Improve Exercise Ontology Across Domains
authors: Peter Berggren, David Myrén, Björn Johansson
source: Proceedings of the ... International ISCRAM Conference
keywords: Terminology; Ontology; Computer science; Data science; Knowledge management; Linguistics; Epistemology; Philosophy
abstract: Scenario-based research is widely used across disciplines, from emergency management and healthcare to military strategy and live-action role-playing (LARP). However, inconsistencies in terminology and reporting practices hinder clarity, replicability, and cross-disciplinary collaboration. This study addresses these challenges by developing a standardized glossary to improve scenario documentation in both academic research and professional practice. Using qualitative data from semi-structured interviews with nine professionals across six domains, the study identifies key terminological inconsistencies and categorizes essential scenario components into nine thematic groups, including scenario types, temporal dynamics, immersion mechanisms, and evaluation methods. The resulting glossary enhances clarity, fosters interdisciplinary communication, and supports methodological rigor, enabling more consistent and comparable scenario-based research. By providing a unified lexicon, this study bridges gaps between qualitative and quantitative approaches, facilitating knowledge transfer and improving replicability. Future research should focus on validating and refining the glossary through practical applications in multi-agency exercises and academic studies.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it addresses interdisciplinary challenges in scenario documentation and proposes a standardized glossary for semantic interoperability across disciplines. It serves as an overview of this specific harmonization effort (the glossary). It also diagnoses systemic challenges related to inconsistent terminology and describes the process of how the glossary was developed through interviews and categorization.
institutions: Region of Southern Denmark; Linköping University; Region Östergötland
topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies

title: From Stage to Data : Ontologies for Performing Arts and Linked Open Data
authors: Clarisse Bardiot, Bernard Jacquemin, Antonios Lagarias, Jeanne Fras, Nicolas Foucault, Jacob Hart, Alexandra Beraldin
source: HAL (Le Centre pour la Communication Scientifique Directe)
publisher: Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique
keywords: Linked data; Performing arts; Discoverability; Interoperability; Ontology; Interconnectivity; Focus (optics); Representation (politics)
abstract: The representation and interconnectivity of performing arts data pose significant challenges due to their inherent complexity. As part of the ERC-funded project From Stage to Data, the Digital Turn of Contemporary Performing Arts Historiography (STAGE), this study investigates the development of an ontology capable of ensuring interoperability between existing ontologies and databases, with a specific focus on the adoption of linked open data principles and the Linked Art ontology, which is built upon the CIDOC CRM framework. By addressing these challenges, the project aims to unify fragmented datasets, enhance the discoverability of performing arts data, and foster interdisciplinary research.
rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on developing an ontology for interdisciplinary performing arts data to ensure interoperability and foster interdisciplinary research. It serves as an overview of a specific effort (the development of an ontology within the STAGE project). It does not propose macroscopic frameworks or diagnose challenges broadly, but rather focuses on a specific domain. The paper describes the process of developing this ontology, addressing how it was designed and implemented.

topics: Semantic Web and Ontologies; Digital Humanities and Scholarship; Libraries and Information Services

title: Crosswalking the Dragons: Ontologies and Metadata within the interoperable federated Cultural Heritage Knowledge Graph Ecosystem
authors: Florian Thiery, Anja Gerber
source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
keywords: Metadata; Interoperability; Cultural heritage; Identifier; Semantic Web; Linked data; Geospatial metadata; Semantic interoperability; Ontology
abstract: Archaeological and cultural heritage research increasingly relies on federated data infrastructures containing heterogeneous datasets originating from excavations, collections, laboratory analyses, and research software. Within a federated Knowledge Graph ecosystem (Thiery et al. 2025a; Fischer et al. 2025), such data can be interlinked using Semantic Web technologies. Yet, the metadata describing these resources often adhere to different standards: collection data are frequently modelled with LIDO (Lightweight Information Describing Objects) or CIDOC CRM, software follows CodeMeta or CFF, and generic metadata employ DCAT, DataCite or schema.org. Achieving interoperability between these layers is a persistent challenge, especially when archaeological data meet interdisciplinary contexts. In archaeology, CIDOC CRM has become the de facto reference model for structuring cultural heritage information and ensuring semantic interoperability. However, when used as the sole framework, it cannot fully represent the analytical or quantitative dimensions of interdisciplinary research, such as laboratory results, or research software. Consequently, domain-specific ontologies and metadata standards must be aligned through crosswalks that extend beyond traditional museum documentation. To address these needs, e.g., initiatives within the German research data infrastructure for human remains of the last 2.6 mio. years, NFDI4Objects, develop integrated solutions for FAIR and LOUD data across the humanities and natural sciences. Central to this effort is the NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph, which adopts CIDOC CRM as a backbone while interlinking to Wikidata and other Wikibase instances as community-driven semantic resources as well as FAIR Digital Objects (FDO). These federated components enable open collaboration, reproducibility, and shared identifiers across institutional and citizen-science boundaries. This paper introduces two complementary frameworks for semantic alignment: the Object Core Metadata Profile (OCMDP), connecting object and software metadata to DCAT, schema.org, and DataCite; and the Material Cultural Heritage Crosswalk Ontology (MaCHeCO), providing ontology-based mappings from archaeological, geoscientific, and community models (such as Wikidata) to CIDOC CRM and its extensions. Together, they support interoperable, interdisciplinary Knowledge Graphs uniting data from cultural heritage, geosciences, and computational archaeology. The research addresses three guiding questions: How can heterogeneous metadata be harmonised through ontology-based crosswalks? How can collections, software, and analytical data be semantically aligned within federated KGs? Which interoperability models best sustain reproducible, interdisciplinary research across archaeology and related sciences? This research combines metadata harmonisation (examples A), ontology engineering (examples B) and applied semantic modelling within a federated Knowledge Graph architecture. The OCMDP ensures compatibility with the emerging NFDI Core Metadata Profile by providing mappings to schema.org, DataCite, DCAT, and CodeMeta, thus bridging between object-oriented cultural-heritage data and research-software metadata. A1. In the first use case, software-related metadata were modelled using CodeMeta and cross-walked to Citation File Format (CFF), Wikidata, and repository metadata (e.g. Zenodo, GitHub). This workflow demonstrates how software can be treated as a first-class FAIR Digital Object (FDO) and research artifact (Thiery et al. 2025b) within archaeological research infrastructures. A2. The second use case applies the OCMDP to museum and collection data using the Minimum Record Recommendation (MDS v1.1) developed by the Minimum Data Recommendation Working Group. Here, collection-level metadata were expressed as RDF entities and harmonised with DCAT and CIDOC CRM concepts, enabling inter-repository querying within the NFDI ecosystem. The MaCHeCO ontology extends the OCMDP by creating a semantic translation layer across multiple disciplinary standards. B1. In the object-biography use case, excavation, collection, conservation, and analytical events were modelled as CIDOC CRM instances, forming a coherent lifecycle representation of artefacts. Implemented within a WissKI-based KG prototype, this model demonstrates how provenance and transformation processes can be linked to OCMDP modules. B2. The LIDO-to-CIDOC CRM mapping establishes correspondences between LIDO elements and CRM classes, enabling museums and archives to publish interoperable RDF data directly consumable by the NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph. B3. The Archaeo-Natural Ontology (ArNO), based on the LADO ontology from archaeology.link, will integrate archaeo-natural datasets, e.g., from the Poseidon, Arbodat+ and PANGAEA platforms, through an ontology crosswalk linking biological sample metadata to CIDOC CRM. This demonstrates how laboratory data and contextual archaeological information can coexist within a shared semantic framework. B4. A further group of use cases explores community-curated data. Data from Wikidata, fuzzy-sl Wikibase, and FactGrid are integrated into the MaCHeCO ontology through semi-automated RDF pipelines. Three examples illustrate the versatility of this workflow: the Ogham Stones dataset, modelling person–tribe networks; the Irish Holy Wells project, representing ritual and spatial relations; and the Cuneiform Mathematical Tablets , linking CDLI artefacts and mathematical formulae. These mappings preserve provenance qualifiers and demonstrate cross-domain interoperability between historical, linguistic, and mathematical knowledge. B5. Finally, the geoscientific use case links archaeological sites with geological observations, exemplified by the Campanian Ignimbrite eruption dataset. Using the Fuzzy Spatial Locations Ontology (FSL-O), spatial uncertainty and precision metadata were modelled alongside site and sampling information, thereby aligning GeoSPARQL, CRMsci, and CRMarchaeo concepts within the MaCHeCO framework. Together, these use cases establish a reproducible methodology for transforming heterogeneous cultural-heritage, scientific, and community data into a CIDOC-aligned, federated Knowledge Graph. The Object Core Metadata Profile (OCMDP) and the Material Cultural Heritage Crosswalk Ontology (MaCHeCO) form complementary components of a federated semantic infrastructure. The OCMDP harmonises software and collection metadata through mappings to DCAT, DataCite, schema.org, and CodeMeta, while MaCHeCO aligns LIDO, ArNO, Wikidata, and GeoSPARQL models with CIDOC CRM. Applied to use cases such as Ogham, Holy Wells, Cuneiform Maths, and Campanian Ignimbrite, both frameworks enable interoperable, cross-disciplinary querying and integration within the NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph. The combined application of OCMDP and MaCHeCO shows that metadata interoperability can be achieved through federated, ontology-driven modelling. OCMDP bridges humanities- and software-based data standards, while MaCHeCO ensures semantic crosswalks across archaeological, geoscientific, and community models. Together, they demonstrate how FAIR and LOUD principles can be operationalised in federated Knowledge Graphs. These frameworks establish a sustainable blueprint for interdisciplinary data integration, provenance tracking, and uncertainty modelling within the broader NFDI Knowledge Graph Federation. rationale: The paper is in scope as it focuses on interdisciplinary semantic interoperability and metadata harmonization for research data across humanities and natural sciences. It introduces and describes specific frameworks (OCMDP and MaCHeCO) for semantic alignment within the NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph. These frameworks are presented as a generalized blueprint for interdisciplinary data integration, addressing systemic challenges. Finally, the paper details the implementation of these frameworks through multiple use cases, demonstrating the methodology and process of achieving cross-disciplinary interoperability.
